stories.headline: Wildfire Crosses Oklahoma Border Into Baca County, Forces Evacuation of Campo
stories.summary:
A fast-moving wildfire crossed into Colorado from Oklahoma at 12:47 p.m. Sunday, prompting mandatory evacuation orders for the town of Campo in Baca County near the state's southeast corner. The fire, now designated the Sharpe Fire, prompted Gov. Polis to issue an emergency declaration activating the State Emergency Operations Plan and directing the Department of Public Safety to oversee all response, recovery and mitigation efforts.

A 77-mile stretch of U.S. 287 was closed from the Oklahoma state line, about eight miles south of Campo, north to U.S. 50 in Lamar, according to the Colorado Department of Transportation. The evacuation order for Campo was lifted Sunday night after fire crews worked to slow the blaze's advance.

The Sharpe Fire is part of a broader stretch of wildfire activity affecting southeastern Colorado as dry, windy conditions persist across the region. The emergency declaration gives state agencies expanded authority to marshal resources for firefighting and community support in Baca County.

stories.headline: One Dead, One Seriously Injured in Fatal Crash on Northbound I-25 in Denver
stories.summary:
One person was killed and another was seriously injured in a two-vehicle crash Sunday morning on northbound Interstate 25 in Denver, according to the Denver Police Department. Officers first reported the crash in an HOV lane near 20th Street at 7:33 a.m.

The driver who died was pronounced dead at the scene and will be identified by the Denver Office of the Medical Examiner. Paramedics transported the second driver to a hospital with serious injuries. The cause of the crash had not been determined as of Sunday.

The fatal collision adds to a series of deadly incidents on Denver-area interstates in recent weeks. Police said the investigation was ongoing and additional details would be released as they become available.

stories.headline: Winter Storm Brings Mountain Snow, Freeze Watch, and Rain to Denver Metro Monday
stories.summary:
A strong cold front moved into Colorado Sunday night, triggering a winter weather advisory for the northern and central mountains through 6 p.m. Monday. Forecasters with the National Weather Service are calling for 4 to 15 inches of snow above 9,000 feet, with the highest totals expected in areas including Rocky Mountain National Park, Summit County and the Indian Peaks.

Denver and the I-25 corridor are expected to see widespread rain Monday with temperatures dropping 20 to 30 degrees from Sunday's highs. A few wet snowflakes could mix in during late Monday afternoon or evening, though no accumulation is expected at lower elevations. The northern I-25 corridor could receive up to 1.5 inches of rain.

A freeze watch was issued for the northeast quarter of Colorado, including Denver, Fort Collins and Boulder, from midnight Monday through Tuesday morning. Lows could approach 30 degrees in some areas, threatening late-season vegetation. Conditions are expected to gradually improve by the weekend, with temperatures returning to the 70s and low 80s.

stories.headline: Thunderstorms Delay More Than 550 Flights at Denver International Airport Sunday
stories.summary:
Afternoon thunderstorms swept across Colorado's Eastern Plains on Sunday, causing significant disruptions at Denver International Airport. As of 6:25 p.m., 551 flights had been delayed and three canceled, according to flight tracking service FlightAware.

Flights were averaging 48-minute delays earlier in the afternoon due to a thunderstorm-driven FAA ground delay at DIA, which was lifted by approximately 6:30 p.m. Southwest Airlines led all carriers with 211 delayed flights, followed by United with 163 and SkyWest with 82. Frontier, Delta and American Airlines also experienced significant delays.

United, Southwest and Icelandair each canceled one flight. The disruptions coincided with the tail end of the Colfax Marathon weekend, when tens of thousands of visitors were in the Denver metro area. Passengers were advised to check with their carriers for updated departure times.

stories.headline: Denver Police Respond to Cherry Creek Mall After Crowd Rushes Swatch Store at Watch Launch
stories.summary:
Denver police officers were called to Cherry Creek Shopping Center at approximately 7:35 a.m. Saturday after reports of a disturbance tied to the global launch of Swatch's new Bioceramic Royal Pop Collection. The mall was cleared out and no arrests were made, according to Denver Police Department spokesperson Sgt. Jay Casillas.

Videos posted to social media appeared to show people swarming the Denver location in hopes of purchasing one of the limited watches, a collaboration between Swatch and luxury watchmaker Audemars Piguet. The eight colorful designs retail for $400 to $420, but hundreds of units were listed on resale websites for thousands of dollars within hours of the launch.

Swatch closed dozens of its stores globally on Saturday, citing extraordinarily high demand and safety concerns for customers and staff. The Cherry Creek location remained closed Sunday. Similar scenes unfolded across the country, including reports of pepper spray use by police at a Long Island mall and at least one arrest in New York City.

stories.headline: Longmont Emergency Unit, Founded in 1957, Faces Uncertain Future Due to Funding and Volunteer Shortages
stories.summary:
The Longmont Emergency Unit, an all-volunteer search and rescue organization that has served Longmont and Boulder County since 1957, is facing a financial and staffing crisis that threatens its continued existence. The unit, which handles roughly 200 to 300 calls per year including water rescues, traffic crash response and evidence recovery, has shrunk from about 55 members at its peak to approximately 18 today.

Former LEU Chief Mike Anderson said the organization is burning through savings and may soon be unable to pay basic operating costs. "If we don't figure it out, either with donations or other contracts, there is a reality that we may not be here in the future," Anderson said. The group's dive rescue team was one of the first in Colorado when it was established following the 1957 drowning of three brothers at Izaak Walton Pond.

The unit has responded to major regional emergencies including the 2013 floods and the 2021 Marshall Fire, and assisted with a fatal hit-and-run in Erie as recently as February. Leaders say that as local police and fire departments have grown larger and better funded, demand for certain LEU services has diminished, making it harder to justify volunteer time and attract new members.

stories.headline: Avalanche Open Western Conference Final Against Vegas Golden Knights at Ball Arena
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche are set to open the Western Conference Final against the Vegas Golden Knights at Ball Arena, having advanced through the playoffs following a strong postseason run. The Avalanche enter the series as favorites to win the Stanley Cup, according to the Denver Post.

Colorado GM Chris MacFarland has drawn widespread recognition for overhauling the roster over the past two seasons, including the high-profile trade that sent Mikko Rantanen to Carolina in exchange for Martin Necas, Jack Drury and two draft picks. The restructured team led the NHL in fewest goals allowed this season, earning the William Jennings Trophy for goaltenders Scott Wedgewood and Mackenzie Blackwood, who were acquired for a combined cap hit of $6.75 million.

Necas has been a key offensive contributor in the playoffs, while Drury has anchored what analysts describe as one of the NHL's best fourth lines. MacFarland's offseason additions of Brock Nelson, Brett Kulak and Nicolas Roy have given Colorado depth across all four lines heading into the conference final.

stories.headline: Diamondbacks Rout Rockies 8-6 in Series Finale at Coors Field
stories.summary:
Corbin Carroll homered twice and drove in three runs as the Arizona Diamondbacks defeated the Colorado Rockies 8-6 on Sunday at Coors Field, winning the rubber game of a three-game series. The game was delayed one hour and 45 minutes by rain before Arizona broke open the game with five runs in the first four innings.

Michael Soroka improved to 6-2 on the season, striking out eight and allowing two runs over 5 2/3 innings. Colorado starter Michael Lorenzen fell to 2-6, giving up seven runs on nine hits and three walks in 4 2/3 innings. The Rockies mounted a late rally with four runs in the eighth but could not complete the comeback.

TJ Rumfield, Troy Johnston and Willi Castro had three straight two-out doubles in the fourth inning to spark Colorado's offense. The Rockies return to action Monday at home against the Texas Rangers, with Jose Quintana scheduled to start opposite MacKenzie Gore.

stories.headline: Broncos Building Pipeline from University of Illinois Under Sean Payton
stories.summary:
The Denver Broncos have quietly developed a recruiting pipeline from the University of Illinois under head coach Sean Payton, selecting three Fighting Illini players in the past four NFL drafts. The latest is Miles Scott, a seventh-round safety taken in April's draft who converted from receiver and was once offered a scholarship by Yale.

Scott joins offensive lineman Alex Palczewski, drafted in 2023, and wide receiver Pat Bryant, taken in 2025, as Broncos players drawn directly from Illinois. Team officials and Illinois coaches say the connection stems from a shared philosophy in evaluating character and football intelligence over pure athleticism or recruiting star ratings.

Illinois receivers coach Justin Stepp noted that Denver scouts asked many of the same character-based questions the Illini coaching staff asks when recruiting high school players. Scott, who became fluent in Spanish in college and conducted weekly one-on-one film sessions with a defensive analyst, is described by former teammates as exceptionally dedicated to learning both on and off the field.

stories.headline: Denver Restaurant Industry Under Pressure as Menu Prices Hit Highest Inflation Rate in Nation
stories.summary:
Denver's restaurant industry continues to face severe financial strain, with menu prices up an average of 28 percent since 2019 — 5.1 percentage points above the national average and the highest restaurant inflation rate in the country, according to the Denver Restaurant Liaison Project. A Colorado Sun opinion column by food writer Carman detailed the mounting closures and survival challenges facing local restaurateurs.

Recent casualties include Wash Park Social and the longtime Imperial Chinese restaurant on South Broadway. A sprawling city-owned space that once housed Pizza Republica has sat vacant for two to three years with no takers. At the same time, wages have risen 50 percent since 2019, forcing some operators to cut staff and shift to self-service ordering and payment.

The stakes extend beyond individual businesses. The State of Denver Restaurants report notes that restaurants contribute 13 percent of the city's sales tax revenue and account for 8.3 percent of local jobs, making the sector critical to Denver's broader economy and its convention and tourism industries. Alcohol sales, historically a key profit margin for restaurants, have also declined, further squeezing operators.

weather.notable: About 24 degrees below the average high for this time of year.

joke.joke: I went to a restaurant and the waiter said, 'How's everything tasting?' I said, 'With my tongue.'

topStory.headline: Trump Warns Iran 'Clock Is Ticking' as Peace Talks Stall and Military Options Loom
topStory.summary:
President Donald Trump issued a stark warning to Iran on Sunday, posting on his Truth Social platform that Tehran must move quickly toward a peace agreement or face devastation. "For Iran, the Clock is Ticking, and they better get moving, FAST, or there won't be anything left of them," Trump wrote. "TIME IS OF THE ESSENCE!" The message followed a phone call between Trump and Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, during which the two leaders discussed the latest developments in the ongoing conflict.

Negotiations between Washington and Tehran have reached a critical impasse, with Iranian media reporting that the United States offered no meaningful concessions in its latest response to Iran's proposed terms. According to Iran's semi-official Fars news agency, Washington presented a five-point list that included demands for Iran to operate only a single nuclear site and transfer its stockpile of highly enriched uranium to the United States. Iran's demands, relayed through Pakistani mediators, reportedly included an immediate ceasefire across all fronts, an end to the U.S. naval blockade of Iranian ports, guarantees against further strikes, compensation for war damage, and recognition of Iranian sovereignty over the Strait of Hormuz.

Trump's national security team is expected to meet Tuesday to discuss potential military options against Iran, according to a report by Axios. The warning came days after Trump declared the existing ceasefire — in effect since early April — was on "massive life support" and dismissed Iranian counteroffers as "totally unacceptable." Earlier in the week, Trump also signaled a possible shift in position, suggesting he could accept a 20-year suspension of Iran's nuclear program rather than demanding its complete dismantlement.

The broader regional situation remained volatile over the weekend. A drone strike struck the perimeter of the United Arab Emirates' sole nuclear power plant, sparking a fire with no reported injuries or radiological release. The UAE called the attack a "dangerous escalation" and said it was investigating whether Iran or its proxies were responsible. In Lebanon, Israeli airstrikes killed seven people, including an Islamic Jihad commander and his teenage daughter, even as a 45-day ceasefire extension remained nominally in place and Hezbollah declared U.S.-brokered talks a "dead end."

The conflict is also carrying significant domestic costs for the United States. Pentagon comptroller Jay Hurst told lawmakers last week that the estimated cost of the war has risen to $29 billion, up from an earlier figure of $25 billion. U.S. gas prices have surged more than 50 percent to a national average of $4.51 per gallon, partly due to Iran's continued control of the Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly 20 percent of the world's oil and liquefied natural gas flows. With midterm elections approaching, the Trump administration faces mounting political pressure to bring the conflict to a resolution.
